coredata.dc:title: Conjugated microporous organic polymer as fluorescent chemosensor for detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Chao
Pan, Guanjun
He, Yi
coredata.dc:description: A conjugated microporous organic polymer (TPA-Bp) comprised of triphenylamine (TPA) and 2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-diformaldehyde (Bp) was prepared via the Schiff-base reaction under ambient conditions. TPA-Bp is an amorphous and microporous spherical nanoparticle with very high stability. TPA-Bp suspension in DMF displayed strong fluorescence emission and selective fluorescence quenching response towards Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm presents linear relationship with the concentrations of both Fe3+ and Fe2+ with low detection limits of 1.02 × 10−5 M for Fe3+ and 5.37 × 10−6 M for Fe2+. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) confirm the selective coordination of N atoms of pyridine unit with Fe ions. The fluorescence quenching of TPA-Bp upon the addition of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions can be attributed to the absorption competition quenching (ACQ) mechanism and the energy transfer between TPA-Bp and Fe3+/Fe2+ ions. This work demonstrates that the conjugated microporous polymers are promising candidates as luminescent sensor for detection of the special analytes in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Conjugated microporous polymer
Iron ion
Fluorescence chemosensor
Absorption competition quenching
Energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fluorescence response of TPA-Bp to different metal ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their appreciation to professor, Guang Wang, whom from Northeast Normal University for this guidance support in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of TPA, Bp, and TPA-Bp. (B) The full XPS spectra of TPA-Bp. (C) N 1s XPS spectra of TPA-Bp. (D) PXRD patterns of Bp, TPA, and TPA-Bp. (E) TGA curve of TPA-Bp. (F) SEM image of TPA-Bp.

(A) N2 sorption isotherms for TPA-Bp. Inset: Pore size distribution of TPA-Bp. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of TPA-Bp (0.07 mg/mL) in DMF upon the addition of different metal ions (0.2 mM) in DMF. (λ ex = 290 nm).
Fluorescence intensities of TPA-Bp at 331 nm in DMF upon the addition of different metal ions and (A) Fe3+ or (B) Fe2+, respectively. (λ ex = 290 nm).

(A) and (C): Fluorescence titration spectra of TPA-Bp (0.07 mg/mL) in DMF upon incremental addition of Fe3+ (0–0.4 mM) and Fe2+ (0–0.4 mM), respectively. (B) and (D): Calibration curves of the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm with the concentration of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions, respectively. (λ ex = 290 nm).
(A) and (B): N 1s XPS spectra of TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+.

The molecular structures, frontier orbital shapes and energy levels of free model compound and complex. Color: C (gray), N (blue), H (white), Fe2+ (wathet). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
UV–Vis absorbance spectra of metal ions and TPA-Bp in DMF (0.07 mg/mL). The concentration of the metal ions is 0.2 mM.
Contrast of diverse aspects of TPA-Bp with the other reported iron sensors.
Recovery experiment of Fe3+/Fe2+ in diverse water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The mean values of four duplicated tests.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The relative standard deviations of four tests.

head.ce:title: Conjugated microporous organic polymer as fluorescent chemosensor for detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A conjugated microporous organic polymer (TPA-Bp) comprised of triphenylamine (TPA) and 2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-diformaldehyde (Bp) was prepared via the Schiff-base reaction under ambient conditions. TPA-Bp is an amorphous and microporous spherical nanoparticle with very high stability. TPA-Bp suspension in DMF displayed strong fluorescence emission and selective fluorescence quenching response towards Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm presents linear relationship with the concentrations of both Fe3+ and Fe2+ with low detection limits of 1.02 × 10−5 M for Fe3+ and 5.37 × 10−6 M for Fe2+. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) confirm the selective coordination of N atoms of pyridine unit with Fe ions. The fluorescence quenching of TPA-Bp upon the addition of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions can be attributed to the absorption competition quenching (ACQ) mechanism and the energy transfer between TPA-Bp and Fe3+/Fe2+ ions. This work demonstrates that the conjugated microporous polymers are promising candidates as luminescent sensor for detection of the special analytes in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is the second abundant metal element in the crust and plays an important role in various biological and environmental systems [1]. Physiologically, Fe(III) and Fe(II) play important roles as redox pairs in humans and biological organisms [2]. Iron ion is not only an essential component of human cells, it also has hematopoietic function and is the core part of hemoglobin, which participates in oxygen storage and electron transport during RNA and DNA synthesis [3–5]. However, excessive or insufficient iron in the body can lead to various diseases. If iron is deficient, people will have iron deficiency anemia, which also cause a decline in immune function and metabolic disorders. However, iron overdose will also be prone to cause the diseases, such as cirrhosis, diabetes and skin brown pigmentation, heart failure and so on [6–10]. China's drinking water sanitation institutions clearly stipulate that the iron content in drinking water does not exceed 0.3 mg/L [11], since it is of great significance to monitor the iron content in biological and drinking water.

Over the past few decades, many efforts have been made to detect iron, and many detection methods have been developed, such as fluorescence spectrophotometry [12,13], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [14], colorimetricanalysis [15], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [16], electrochemical methods [17] and atomic spectroscopy [18]. Among which, the fluorescence analysis methods have attracted wide attention because of their high selectivity, high responsiveness and low detection limits [19–21]. At present, the main materials used for fluorescence analysis to detect iron ions are organic small molecules [12,22,23], metal organic frameworks (MOF) [24,25], graphene quantum dots [26], porous organic polymers [27] and so on.

Porous organic polymers, as a new material, have attracted the research interest of chemical workers in recent years. Compared with inorganic materials, it has the virtues of low density, high specific surface area and tunable pore size [28,29]. It is widely used in photoelectricity [30,31], catalysis [32,33], sensing [34,35], gas adsorption and separation [36]. Porous organic polymers can be divided into two categories according to whether they have crystalline structure, including covalent organic frameworks with crystalline forms [37–39] and amorphous porous organic polymers without crystalline forms, such as hyper-cross-linked polymers (HCPs) [40,41], covalent organic polymers (COPs) [42], conjugated microporous polymers (CMPs) [43,44] and porous aromatic frameworks (PAFs) [45,46]. Because amorphous porous organic polymers lose their crystallinity in comparison with covalent organic frameworks and weaken the π-π interaction between molecular layers [47], the fluorescence properties of polymers is improved.

Herein, a conjugated microporous polymer (TPA-Bp) comprised of triphenylamine (TPA) and 2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-diformaldehyde (Bp) was prepared via the Schiff-base reaction under ambient conditions as shown in Fig. 1 . TPA-Bp possessed porous structure and high thermal stability. TPA-Bp displayed obvious fluorescence quenching response for the selective sensing of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in DMF. The absorption competition quenching (ACQ) and energy transfer mechanism between TPA-Bp and iron ions are the reasons of fluorescence quenching. This work explored a new conjugated porous polymer as fluorescence chemosensor to recognize and detect Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions in the physiological biological and environmental samples.

Unless otherwise mentioned, materials and reagents were obtained from commercial channels and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water used in all measurement is from Milli-Q water purification system. Elemental analysis data was obtained on a Euro Vector EA3000 elemental analyzer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were taken on a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Quantum 2000× system. PXRD patterns measurements were carried out on a Rigaku SmartLab diffractometer. The morphology of the TPA-Bp was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philips XL30). Thermogravimetric (TGA) curves were recorded on a Diamond TG/DTA analyzer with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under air flow. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were obtained at 77 K with an Autosorb iQ2 adsorptometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra date was from a Varian Cary 500 Spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-diformaldehyde (Bp) (31.83 mg, 0.15 mmol) and triphenylamine (TPA) (29.0 mg, 0.1 mmol) were dissolved in 2.0 mL of 1,4-dioxane in a 10 mL glass vial. Acetic acid (3.0 M, 0.2 mL) was added after sonication for 10 min, then the vial was sealed and kept still for 3 days under ambient conditions. The precipitate generated was collected by filtration, washed thoroughly with DMF, acetone, and THF. Then the product was dried in vacuum at 70 °C for 10 h to yield TPA-Bp as a brick red powder (90% yield). The synthetic route and molecular structure of TPA-Bp is shown in Fig. 1.

Preparation of TPA-Bp stock solution: 10 mg TPA-Bp were dissolved in 150 mL DMF and dispersed evenly under ultrasonic for 3 min. The stock solutions (20 mM) of metal ions including K+, Na+, Ag+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Zn2+ and Fe2+ were also prepared by dissolving their salts in ultrapure water, respectively. Each of the above metal ion solutions (50 μL) was respectively added to the TPA-Bp solution (5 mL) during the selective experiment.

First, the chemical structure of TPA-Bp was characterized by FTIR spectra. The FTIR spectra of TPA-Bp and two monomers are compared and shown in Fig. 2 A. The two absorption peaks of TPA at 3406 cm−1 and 3336 cm−1 can be assigned to N–H vibration peak changed from obvious spikes to broad and blunt in TPA-Bp, which indicates that the amino groups of the monomer TPA has been consumed in the reaction process. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1697 cm−1 of Bp can be ascribed to the characteristic peak of CO significantly weakened in TPA-Bp, also confirms that occurring of reaction of Bp with TPA. More important, the new absorption peak at 1619 cm−1 attributed to the characteristic stretching band of –CN- demonstrates that the amine of TPA has reacted with aldehyde group of Bp [48].

Second, the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) elemental analysis further confirmed the formation of –CN- bond and elemental composition of TPA-Bp. The whole XPS spectrum of TPA-Bp (Fig. 2B) indicates the existence of C, N and O elements in TPA-Bp, O element is ascribed to the unreacted aldehyde groups. Elemental analysis results proved the contents of C, N and H are basically consistent with the actual component in TPA-Bp. The test contents of C, N and H in TPA-Bp were respective 69.937%, 16.549%, and 7.591%, the theoretical values calculated from the molecular components of TPA-Bp (C72H48N14)n (C, 77.98%; N, 17.69%; H, 4.33%). In addition, the peak of N 1s at 398.3 eV in the XPS spectrum of TPA-Bp is attributed to the CN binding energy (Fig. 2C) and also confirms the formation of the chemical bonds (CN) in the TPA-Bp.

Comparing of PXRD spectra of TPA-Bp, TPA and Bp also illustrated the happening of reaction TPA and Bp, shown in Fig. 2D. The obvious sharp diffraction peaks on the PXRD spectra of TPA and Bp indicate that TPA and Bp is crystalline, but these peaks disappeared in the spectra of TPA-Bp indicating these two monomers has reacted with each other. On the contrary, there is only one wide peak at ∼19° proving TPA-Bp is amorphous powder.

The thermal stability of TPA-Bp was appraised by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), shown in Fig. 2E, TPA-Bp remained stable below 430 °C with a weight loss of about 15%. After 430 °C, TPA-Bp began to decompose fast and decomposed completely till 640 °C. The result of TGA proves TPA-Bp has very high stability.

The morphology of TPA-Bp was observed with scanning electron microscope (SEM). TPA-Bp particles are made up of 100 nm spheres, shown in Fig. 2F. The molecular structure of TPA-Bp is centered at TPA unit and expands in a symmetrical structure with the dendritic structure. When different layers of molecule crisscrossed together to form sphere, pores formed and are left inside the sphere. Therefore, N2 adsorption-desorption test was conducted to investigate the porosity and surface area of TPA-Bp, the results are shown in Fig. 3 A. TPA-Bp has a low Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 20.509 m2g-1, and the calculation on the pore size distribution on the basis of the non-local density functional theory suggests that TPA-Bp is a porous material and the most of pore size is distributed at 2.77 nm.

The effect of pH on the stability of TPA-Bp was investigated, each TPA-Bp (10 mg) sample were respectively dispersed in water (100 mL) with different pH values and their fluorescence intensities at 503 nm were continuously monitored under the excitation of 365 nm every 1 h, the results were shown in Fig. S1. The fluorescence intensities in strongly acidic and alkaline water solutions are relative larger than that in pH range of 4～10. All the fluorescence intensities at all test pH values except for pH = 2 displayed slight increase after 1 h and then remained unchanged within 4 h. At pH = 2, the fluorescence intensity remarkably increased after first hour and then also kept constant. These results illustrated that pH has no obvious effect on the stability of TPA-Bp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of TPA-Bp to different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: TPA is often used as fluorescence material and Bp has strong chelating N atoms, additionally, Bp connects to TPA via –CN double bond, so TPA-Bp is a fluorophore with a large conjugation system. Therefore, the change of electron distribution of Bp unit after coordination with metal ions has effect on the fluorescence property of TPA-Bp. Based on the above statements, the fluorescence response towards metal ions was tested.

The size of TPA-Bp particle is 100 nm and not large, so TPA-Bp is easy to disperse in many kinds of solvents. The fluorescence property of TPA-Bp (0.07 mg/mL) in DMF solvent was investigated and compared with that of TPA and Bp (1 × 10−5 mol/L). TPA-Bp shows an intense fluorescence emission with a peak at 331 nm under the excitation of 290 nm light (Fig. 3B), which has large difference with those of TPA (443 nm) and Bp (410 nm) (Fig. S2). This different fluorescence band indicates the fluorescence of TPA-Bp is from the whole molecule conjugation but not only TPA or Bp group. The polarity of solvents has large effect on the fluorescence property and fluorescence response to different analytes, so the different solvents such as dioxane, ethanol and DMF were used as solvent to investigate the fluorescence response of TPA-Bp towards metal ions, including K+, Na+, Ag+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Zn2+ and Fe2+. 50.0 μL solutions of different metal ions (20 mM) were added into 5 mL TPA-Bp solutions, respectively, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after the mixing of solutions for 3 min. TPA-Bp in dioxane and ethanol presented irregular response towards the metal ions, the changes of spectra was shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4. It is interesting that TPA-Bp in DMF displayed highly selective fluorescence quenching responses towards Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions, the additions of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions caused the obvious fluorescence quenching, but the most added other metal ions just impacted a little perturbation effect on the fluorescence spectra of TPA-Bp, shown in Fig. 3B. The above experimental results show that TPA-Bp has high selectivity of fluorescence response towards Fe2+ and Fe3+ in DMF and can be used to recognize the existence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. The selective fluorescence response of TPA-Bp towards Fe2+ and Fe3+ can be attributed to the selective coordination of Bp unit with Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, which can be attributed to the synergetic effect of the special pore structure in the sphere of TPA-Bp and the chelating effect of Bp unit. The special spatial structure just permits Fe2+ and Fe3+ ion to access and coordinates with N atoms of Bp. The other metal ions can't enter the pore of TPA-Bp and not coordinate with Bp unit and not further give an obvious effect on the fluorescence of TPA-Bp. This assumption has been proved by the following experiment, 2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-diformaldehyde (Bp) has no selective fluorescence response towards the aforementioned metal ions, shown in Fig. S5. Upon the addition of these metal ions (20 equiv. of Bp), the fluorescence spectra of Bp (1 × 10−5 mol/L) in DMF displayed enhancement or quenching effect in a large extent and has no regularity. This result indicates that Bp can coordination with the most metal ions but has no special selectivity.

The fast fluorescence response is conducive to the real-time detection of chemosensors. The changes of the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm in DMF after the addition of Fe3+ or Fe2+ are illustrated in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7. The fluorescence intensity fast decreased the lowest equilibrium points whether addition Fe3+ or Fe2+ within 5 s, indicating TPA-Bp has very fast fluorescence response to Fe3+/Fe2+ ions and is suitable for the real-time recognition and detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions.

Competitive experiment is one of the indicators to test whether Fe3+ and Fe2+ can resist the interference of other coexisting metal ions. Therefore, the same amount of each competing metal ions and Fe3+ or Fe2+ were added synchronously into the dispersion of TPA-Bp in DMF, their fluorescence spectra were recorded and compared with that of TPA-Bp dispersion only containing the competitive metal ion. The changes of the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm upon the different metal ions are shown in Fig. 4 . The fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp dispersion containing the competitive metal ion and Fe3+ or Fe2+ is almost same as that only containing Fe3+ or Fe2+, which indicates that the coexistence of the aforementioned metal ions does not influence the quenching effect of Fe3+ or Fe2+ on the fluorescence of TPA-Bp. In other word, TPA-Bp possesses strong anti-interference ability in the process of detecting Fe3+ and Fe2+, and can be used to identify and detect Fe3+ and Fe2+ in real samples.

To investigate the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp and the concentration of Fe2+ and Fe3+, fluorescence titration experiments were carried out to evaluate the sensing characteristics of TPA-Bp for Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence spectra of TPA-Bp gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ and Fe2+ concentration (Fig. 5 A and C). The fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm with the concentration of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in the range of 0.04–0.32 mM for Fe3+ and 0–0.2 mM for Fe2+ showed good linearity and high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9927 for Fe3+ and 0.9873 for Fe2+) (Fig. 5B and D). Furthermore, the low detection limits (LOD = 3σ/κ) of TPA-Bp for Fe2+ and Fe3+ were calculated as to be 5.37 × 10−6 M for Fe2+ and 1.02 × 10−5 M for Fe3+respectively [49]. Here, σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity at 331 nm of the blank solution, κ is the slope of the calibration curves.

To further study the effects of Fe3+ and Fe2+ on fluorescence of TPA-Bp, the quenching coefficient (Ksv) of Fe3+ and Fe2+ on fluorescence was calculated based on the Stern-Volmer equation [50]: I0/I = 1+Ksv [M], respectively, the Stern-Volmer curves are shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. Where I and I0 are the fluorescent intensity of TPA-Bp at 331 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions; [M] is the metal ion concentration. The KSV values for TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+ were obtained by fitting of the experimental data based on the Stern-Volmer curves to be 2.22 × 103 M−1 and 4.09 × 103 M−1, respectively. The large quenching coefficients indicate the strong affinity of TPA-Bp with Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions. Compared with the reported fluorescence chemosensors for iron ions, the sensing performance of TPA-Bp including detection limit, linearity range and response time ranked the best among them, as shown in Table 1 . The high sensitivity of TPA-Bp towards Fe3+/Fe2+ indicated that TPA-Bp could be applied to qualitatively and quantitatively detect Fe3+/Fe2+ at very low concentration.

To demonstrate the feasibility of TPA-Bp to detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in real samples, the recovery test of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was performed in different water samples including tap water and drinking water. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of Fe3+ and Fe2+ were 98.12–101.5% and 95.25–100.2%, respectively. These results indicate that TPA-Bp has satisfactory result in detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ samples with different concentrations. The results proved that TPA-Bp had sufficient sensitivity as a fluorescence chemosensor for the identification and detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ both in chemical and environmental samples.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of TPA-Bp upon the interaction with Fe3+ or Fe2+ was explored with the FTIR, XPS and UV–vis spectra. The samples of TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+ for FTIR and XPS measurements were prepared as following: 300 mg TPA-Bp samples were immersed in 20 mL water solutions of Fe3+ or Fe2+, respectively, after 2 h, the TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+ were filtered and fully washed with water and DMF in turn. Then, the samples were dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 5 h. As shown in Fig. S10, there is no obvious wavenumber change of the vibration peak of C = N of Schiff base in TPA-Bp molecule after the coordination of Fe ions with TPA-Bp, proving that the N atom of C = N of Schiff base did not take part in the coordination between Fe3+ or Fe2+ and TPA-Bp. The interaction of TPA-Bp with Fe3+ or Fe2+ was also proved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 6 A and B). After complexing of TPA-Bp with Fe3+ or Fe2+ ion, the two N 1s peaks at 399.50 and 398.30 eV of C–N and CN in the XPS spectrum of TPA-Bp (Fig. 2C) also appeared in the spectra of TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+ [59]. Meanwhile, a new peak of N 1s at 399.2 eV also emerged in the spectra of both TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+ samples, which are attributed to Fe–N bond and confirms the existence of interaction between TPA-Bp and Fe3+ or Fe2+ ion. The peak intensities of N 1s of CN bond at 398.3 eV in the XPS spectra of TPA-Bp@Fe3+ and TPA-Bp@Fe2+ also decreased, which is influenced by the interaction of N atom of bipyridine with Fe3+ or Fe2+ ion [48]. In addition, a new peak of N 1s at 405.4 eV in the spectrum of TPA-Bp@Fe3+ corresponding to N 1s of –NO3 group could be clearly observed, which indirectly confirms the bonding of Fe3+ with TPA-Bp due the usage of Fe(NO3)3 in the preparation of TPA-Bp@Fe3+ [48]. These above XPS results demonstrate that Fe3+ or Fe2+ ion can coordinate with the N atoms of Bp unit of TPA-Bp.

In order to further interpret the coordination and complex mode of TPA-Bp with Fe2+/Fe3+, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the model compound of TPA-Bp with Fe2+ ion as an example were carried out based on the B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of Gaussian 09 package. The complex of model compound with Fe2+ ion was optimized at the B3LYP level of density functional theory, together with vibrational frequency calculations to obtain minima of energy. The 6-31G (d,p) basis set was used to describe the C, H, O and N atoms. The optimized orbital energy levels and orbital distribution of free model compound and complex were shown in Fig. 7 . The complexation reaction was realized by coordination of two N atoms of bipyridine with Fe2+. On the free model compound, the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) is mainly distributed on two triphenylamine units and little on bipyridine group. On the contrast, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO) focused on the bipyridine group. The band gap between HOMO (−5.27 eV) and LUMO (−2.61 eV) was calculated as 2.66 eV. After coordination with Fe2+, the HOMO of complex is still distributed on two triphenylamine units, but the LUMO is concentrated on the complex part of N atoms and Fe2+ ion. Meanwhile, the band gap between HOMO and LUMO of complex (1.84 eV) is obviously lower than that of free model compound, which also indicates the formation of more stable complex. The DFT calculation further illustrates the complexing mode of TPA-Bp with Fe2+.

The effect of Fe3+ and Fe2+ on TPA-Bp fluorescence can be attributed to the absorption competition quenching (ACQ) [60,61] and energy transfer mechanism. As shown in Fig. 8 , Fe3+ and Fe2+ has strong UV absorption in the range of 250–400 nm, while other metal ions have no UV absorption. The UV absorption bands of Fe3+ and Fe2+ overlap well with the fluorescence band of TPA-Bp, since the emitting energy of TPA-Bp at excited state transfers to Fe3+ or Fe2+ ion and cause the decrease of fluorescence spectra, this is energy transfer mechanism. On the other hand, the excitation wavelength used in fluorescence measurement is 290 nm, both Fe3+ and Fe2+ ion also strongly absorb excitation light of 290 nm, which decreases the excitation energy absorbed by TPA-Bp for fluorescence emitting, thus the fluorescence intensity is reduced. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching is also closely related to the reported absorption competition quenching (ACQ) mechanism. Based on the above experimental results, TPA-Bp can selectively coordinate with Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions via the N atoms of bipyridine, and then the energy transfer and ACQ mechanisms are responsible for the fluorescence quenching.

In conclusion, we successfully prepared a conjugated microporous organic polymer (TPA-Bp) with triphenylamine and bipyridine by Schiff-base condensation reaction. TPA-Bp is amorphous and porous spherical nanoparticle and has very high thermal stability. TPA-Bp displayed highly selective fluorescence quenching in response to Fe3+ and Fe2+ with very short response time. The fluorescence intensity of TPA-Bp presents a linear relationship with the concentrations of both Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions, the low detection limits were 1.02 × 10−5 M for Fe3+ and 5.37 × 10−6 M for Fe2+ ions. In addition, TPA-Bp has good recovery in practical water samples. The specially porous structure endows TPA-Bp the highly selective coordination ability with Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions, the ACQ mechanism and energy transfer are the cause of the fluorescence quenching. This work demonstrates that the conjugated porous polymers have great potential as fluorescence chemosensors to recognize and detect special analytes in practical samples.

Credit authorship contribution statement Yi He designed the study and revise the manuscript, Guanjun Pan assisted the experiment and collected test data, Chao Zhang did the experiment and analyzed test data and wrote the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their appreciation to professor, Guang Wang, whom from Northeast Normal University for this guidance support in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122872.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global iron connections between desert dust, ocean biogeochemistry, and climate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.D. Jickells, Z.S. An, K.K. Andersen, A.R. Baker, G. Bergametti, N. Brooks, J.J. Cao, P.W. Boyd, R.A. Duce, K.A. Hunter, H. Kawahata, N. Kubilay, J. laRoche, P.S. Liss, N. Mahowald, J.M. Prospero, A.J. Ridgwell, I. Tegen, R. Torres, Global iron connections between desert dust, ocean biogeochemistry, and climate, Science 308 (2005) 67-71.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring of Fe (II) ions in living cells using a novel quinoline-derived fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.H. Cai, H.L. Wang, R.J. Man, Monitoring of Fe (II) ions in living cells using a novel quinoline-derived fluorescent probe,Spectrochimica acta. Part A. Molecular and biomolecular spectroscopy 255 (2021) 119729.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of sulfur doped carbon quantum dots for highly selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of Fe(2+) and Fe(3+) ions in oral ferrous gluconate samples
